coredata.dc:title: A rapid and recyclable analysis method for sulfadimethoxine detection based on molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor reinforced by GQDs/ZIF-8 nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Hatamluyi, Behnaz
Sadeghzadeh, Samira
Balouch Zehi, Zakiyeh
Rezayi, Majid
Tavakoly Sany, Seyedeh Belin
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive molecularly imprinted polymeric (MIP) sensor for rapid and specific detection of sulfadimethoxine (SDM) in milk and water samples was developed in this research. The proposed MIP sensor construction process was based on the drop-casting of GQDs on a glassy carbon electrode and electro-polymerization of novel zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) with SDM as template and aniline as monomer. GQDs with excellent conductivity and ZIF-8 with porous structure showed a synergistic effect on promoting electron transfer and increasing the number of binding sites, thereby providing high sensitivity for SDM detection. Under optimal conditions, the sensor showed two dynamic ranges from 0.01 to 1.0 nM and 1.0 to 275 nM with a detection limit of 0.0063 nM. The accuracy and precision of the proposed platform was studied in real samples for four concentration levels and the recoveries were obtained from 96.3 to 107 % with RSD values of 1.87 to 4.11 % (n = 3).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfadimethoxine
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Metal-organic framework
Graphene quantum dots
Electrochemical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of the molar ratio of template to monomer
Effect of the mass ratio of template molecule to ZIF-8
Effect of the number of electropolymerization cycles
Effect of desorption time and rebinding time

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was financially supported by the Deputy of Research, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (Grant No. 971542), Mashhad, Iran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of GQDs, (B) TEM image, (C) SEM image, (D) XRD pattern, (E) BET analyses, and (F) Pore size distribution of ZIF-8.

(A) CVs of different electrodes in 1.0 mM [Fe(CN) 6]/[Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl (a) bare GCE, (b) GQD/GCE, (c) GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE before and (d) after removing the SDM, (e) GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE after 300 s incubation in 10 nM SDM, (f) GQD/MIP/GCE after removing the SDM, and (g) GQD/ZIF-8/NIP/GCE after removing the SDM. (B) EDX image of GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE, (C) SEM image of bare GCE, (D) SEM image of GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE.

(A) DPVs of GQDs/ZIF-8/NIP/GCE at various concentrations of SDM (0, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 5.0, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 225, 250, and 275 nM), (B) and (C) calibration curves correlating ΔI with the concentration of SDM.

(A) The selectivity of the imprinted and non-imprinted sensors for SDM and different interferences. (B) Selectivity evaluation of the proposed MIP sensor for SDM (50 nM), SMX (200 nM), CP (200 nM), FOL (200 nM), TAP (200 nM), CTC (200 nM), STC (200 nM), Mixture.
A schematic preparation of MIP/ZIF-8/GCE and electrochemical detection of SDM.
Recovery of SDMfrom spiked milk and river water samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A rapid and recyclable analysis method for sulfadimethoxine detection based on molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor reinforced by GQDs/ZIF-8 nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Health Education and Health Promotion, Faculty of Health, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences, Mashhad, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An ultrasensitive molecularly imprinted polymeric (MIP) sensor for rapid and specific detection of sulfadimethoxine (SDM) in milk and water samples was developed in this research. The proposed MIP sensor construction process was based on the drop-casting of GQDs on a glassy carbon electrode and electro-polymerization of novel zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) with SDM as template and aniline as monomer. GQDs with excellent conductivity and ZIF-8 with porous structure showed a synergistic effect on promoting electron transfer and increasing the number of binding sites, thereby providing high sensitivity for SDM detection. Under optimal conditions, the sensor showed two dynamic ranges from 0.01 to 1.0 nM and 1.0 to 275 nM with a detection limit of 0.0063 nM. The accuracy and precision of the proposed platform was studied in real samples for four concentration levels and the recoveries were obtained from 96.3 to 107 % with RSD values of 1.87 to 4.11 % (n = 3).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfadimethoxine (SDM) as a sulfonamide antimicrobial has been used in veterinary medicine for a long time [1]. The processing of foodstuff may reduce SDM residue [2] but intake of trace amounts of it would affect human health [3] causing antibiotic resistance [3,4] and toxicity [3]. According to the necessity of monitoring this antibiotic in water and dairy products, some legislation authorities e.g., the government of Canada has defined the maximum residue level for it [5]. SDM has been determined in some matrixes by various analytical procedures and methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [6–8], fluorescence spectrophotometry [3], potentiometric measurements with plastic membrane electrode [9], and visual detection by aptasensor using unmodified gold nanoparticles [10]. Although the mentioned approaches are efficient in SDM determination, some of these methods, such as chromatography have disadvantages e.g., expensive equipment, time-consuming processes, and sophisticated pretreatment procedure [11,12]. On the other hand, removing or suppression of interference effects by minimum treatment of the sample matrix is an interesting need of the analyst which is achievable by molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). MIP synthesis is based on the polymerization of monomers in presence of the target molecules followed by removing the template molecules leading to the creation of selective recognition sites inside the consequent cavities [13,14]. The obtained material cavities are capable to recognize the analyte according to the complement in shape, size and functionality with it due to the three-dimensional arrangement of the binding sites [15]. The MIP separated target molecules have been analyzed by some techniques such as molecular spectrophotometry [16], HPLC-tandem mass spectrometry [17] and differential pulse voltammetry [18]. Electrochemical analyzers are common and have high sensitivity comparable to the best ones [15,19,20].

Electropolymerization is one of the best choices in preparing MIP sensors due to the ability to control the deposition of polymers on the transducer in a precise manner [15]. Moreover, the sensitivity of electrochemically prepared MIP sensors can be improved by adding nanomaterials or metallic compounds in polymerization procedures [15]. Zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) is a porous hydrophobic metal–organic framework (MOF) with rigid structure Zn (II)- single nodes and 2-methylimidazolate (2-MI) ligands [21]. This MOF is a stable compound in hydrothermal and basic conditions [21,22]. ZIF-8 has been proposed to apply as a drug delivery material due to its porous structure [21]. Generally speaking, MOFs are coordination materials with nanotechnology-based applications in food chemistry ranging from antibiotics to heavy metals sensors and/or extractors [23]. Some studies revealed that applications of MOFs in MIPs have improved mass transfer rate and recognition ability [24]. ZIF-8 has been applied as a MIP modifier in electrochemical sensors [25,26] and solid phase extraction sorbent with chromatographic detection [27].

Generally, carbon quantum dots were used for chemical sensing according to their non-toxic behavior, good photo stability, and chemical stability [28]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that have the mentioned valuable properties of CQDs, as well as inertness and surface grafting, are zero-dimensional compounds by converting two-dimensional graphene [29]. GQDs have been used in MIP sensing preparation [30] due to their unique properties.

In this work, a novel ZIF-8/MIP was developed on the surface of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) modified with GQDs. The experimental performance of the final constructed sensor called GQDs/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE was evaluated in SDM determination in milk and water samples. The fabricated sensor demonstrated good analytical capability together with ease of preparation and use. To the best of our knowledge, there is no report of GQDs/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE based on electropolymerized aniline and voltammetric detection of SDM.

Voltammetric analyses were performed using a three-electrode potantiostat/galvanostat (Autolab PGSTAT 101). The electrochemical measurements were done on a GQDs/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE with respect to an Ag/AgCl electrode. The applied auxiliary electrode was a platinum electrode. The surface microscopic images were obtained by TESCAN MIRA3-FEG scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to measure the particle size of the synthesized nanostructures by an ALEO 912 AB TEM. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were studied by a Philips's analytical X-ray instrument. Studies of surface area and porosity of the synthesized MOF were performed based on Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) by a BELSORP-mini II analyzer (Particle Test Pty Ltd, Bel Japan Inc., Japan) using N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms at 77 K in a liquid nitrogen media.

Sulfadimethoxine and 2-methylimidazole (2-MI) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Other materials, Zinc nitrate (Zn (NO3)2·6H2O), glucose, aniline, ethylenediamine, sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), methanol (CH3OH), ethanol (CH3CH2OH), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium ferricyanide [K3Fe(CN)6], potassium ferrocyanide [K4Fe(CN)6], sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium phosphate(Na2HPO4), and phosphoric acid (H3PO4) were procured from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). All reagents and solvents had analytical grades and the reagents used were analytical grades.

Synthesis of GQDs was performed according to the reported methodology by Gu et al., [31]. In brief, 400 μL of ethylenediamine, 200 μL of hydrochloric acid (37 %, w/w) and 1.0 g of glucose were added to DI water (15 mL) and stirred for 30 min. The compound was transferred to an autoclave and the hydrothermal process was accomplished at 200 ˚C for 6 h. The final brown product was centrifuged (10,000 rpm) and dialyzed to delete large portions of GQDs and stored at 4˚C for further use.

ZIF-8 was synthesized as reported by Wang et al., [32]. Briefly, 0.73 g Zn (NO3)2·6H2O and 0.81 g of 2-MI were each added separately in methanol (50 mL). The 2-MI solution was then slowly added to the Zn (NO3)2·6H2O solution until the turbidity solution turned into a milky white solution. The compound was shielded against the light for 24 h. The created suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times with ethanol. Finally, the white ZIF-8 crystals were dried in a vacuum at 40 ˚C.

The surface of a GCE was completely polished and modified with GQDs and ZIF-8/MIP as follows: In the first step, 10 μL of the synthesized GQDs were dropped onto the naked GCE surface and dried at room temperature (RT). To construct the polymeric network, 40 mM of aniline (functional monomer), 30 mM of SDM (template) and 4.0 mg mL−1 ZIF-8 were added to 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) and electro-polymerization was performed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range 0.0 to + 1.4 V, a scan rate of 50 mV s−1, and with 8 cyclic scans. This process is based on the non-covalent MIP and the presence of several intermolecular bonds is expected. The most important and dominant intermolecular possible bond between SDM molecules and MIP film is hydrogen bonding. Eventually, the final MIP modified electrode was plunged in H2SO4 solution (0.1 M) with stirred for 10 min to delete the pattern molecules. The complete removal of the SDM molecules was checked by UV–vis analysis at SDM absorption peak (ʎmax = 270 nm) [33]. Furthermore, the non-imprinted electrode (GQDs/ZIF-8/NIP/GCE) was fabricated with the above process, except addition of SDM molecules. To better understand the influence of GQDs and ZIF-8 on sensor performance, the GQD/GCE and GQD/MIP/GCE were also constructed. The construction process of the MIP sensor was schematically illustrated in Scheme 1 .

In this study, the variations of peak current severity of ferrocyanide probe solution [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was used to check the electrochemical performance of various modified electrodes by CV method. Also, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was utilized to survey SDM concentration in 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.1 M KCl. For this purpose, initially the peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− was recorded by prepared MIP electrode then to rebind the SDM-template, the GQDs/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE was prime put in a solution of SDM for 300 s under stirring at 300 rpm. The stirring was stopped and electrode was washed with DI water to delete the surface-sticked remaining material. Subsequently, the sensor was again employed to perform the DPV test in the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox solution. The difference (ΔI) of the peak current of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in the absence and in the presence of the SDM molecules was used to calculate SDM concentration indirectly. For DPV measurements, the potential range was set as −0.10 to 0.50 V, the scan rate and amplitude were 50 mV s−1 and 50 mV, respectively. After every assay, the GQDs/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE was stirred in 0.1 M H2SO4 for 10 min to delete SDM. In this way, the regained sensor was prepared for the other test.

Because sulfunamides are the main sources of organic contamination of water [34] and can lead to the undesired residue in foodstuff [35], SDM was determined in milk and river water samples. Milk sample was purchased from a local market and prepared based on the work of Song et al., [36]. The river water sample was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for ten minutes and analyzed by the proposed method without any further pretreatment. The prepared samples were also analyzed after being spiked by the appropriate amounts of the SDM stock solution to check the accuracy of the fabricated MIP sensor.
The GQDs were characterized by TEM as indicated in Fig. 1 A. As it can be seen the distribution of disk shape GQDs is uniform. The size of mentioned particles was 4 to 8 nm based on the TEM analysis.

The successful synthesis of ZIF-8 nanoparticles was verified by TEM (Fig. 1B) and SEM (Fig. 1C). The obtained electronic microscopic images of two techniques show the rhombic structure of ZIF-8 and the SEM image demonstrates its polyhedral shape. The characterization of the synthesized ZIF-8 was also performed using XRD and BET analyses. The crystal structure of ZIF-8 was confirmed by diffraction peaks at 2ɵ = 7.40, 10.52, 12.63, 14.80, 16.54, 18.1, which correspond to ZIF-8 crystal planes of (0 1 1), (0 0 2), (1 1 2), (0 2 2), (0 1 3), and (2 2 2) [32] (Fig. 1D). Specific surface area, average pore diameter and pore volume were determined to be 1045.29 m2·g−1, 2.65 nm, and 0.6949 Cm3·g−1 via BET analysis, respectively (Fig. 1E).

The electrochemical behavior of the electrodes were evaluated at different steps of fabrication by CV procedure in a 1.0 mM [Fe(CN) 6]/[Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution containing 0.1 M KCl. As shown in Fig. 2 A following decorating GQDs nanoparticles upon GCE, a significant gain in oxidation/reduction peak currents of ferrocyanide was observed due to ameliorated conductance and larger efficient surface area (curve a, b) [37]. The result of such a treatment is the improvement of sensitivity in the limits of the quantitation concentrations leading to the extension of the dynamic range or at least minimization of the uncertainty. After the formation of the MIP film on the GQD/GCE, the peak currents were sharply reduced, because the redox probe penetrability to the electrically active surface was barricaded (curve c). In the next step, due to the removal of the SDM molecules from the polymeric network, ferrocyanide ions were able to reach the electrode surface through these created fossas and carry out the electron exchange process, causing the signal of redox to return (curve d). After the electrode was immersed in SDM solution (10 nM) for a defined time, the peak currents re-decreased because the fossas were completely engrossed by SDM molecules (curve e). To evaluate the role of ZIF-8 in the efficiency of the sensor, the CV of the GQD/MIP/GCE sensor without ZIF-8 nanoparticles was investigated after deleting SDM molecules. The GQD/MIP/GCE (curve f) was presented lower current intensities as opposed to the GQDs/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE (curve d) which proves that ZIF-8 with its special porous structure has a significant effect on increasing the sensitivity of the MIP sensor. Finally, the GQD/ZIF-8/NIP/GCE was evaluated and showed very low peak currents as expected. This was related to the insulating film and unformed connection places which indicates that there are no cavities that fit the pattern of the SDM molecule. According to these results, the SDM MIP sensor was successfully constructed with high selectivity and sensitivity. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was also used to confirm the successful formation of the polymeric network. EDX analysis can provide elemental identification [38] and the obtained spectrum demonstrated zinc, carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and gold peaks (Fig. 2B). The observed peaks were some of the constructional elements of the polymer, GQD and ZIF-8 in addition to gold which belongs to the surface coating process of EDX spectroscopy. The absence of unrelated elements confirmed the lack of surface contamination and acceptable performance of the electrode modification procedure.

SEM images of the surface of bare GCE and GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE are also presented in Fig. 2C and Fig. 2D, respectively. It could be observed that the bare GCE appeared uniformly and smooth without any porous polymeric texture. The rugged GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE surface was coated with a porous polymeric layer due to the presence of ZIF-8/polyaniline film. Thus the modified electrode (Fig. 2D) showed increased surface area followed by improved electron transfer on the electrode surface. So differences in morphologies between SEM images confirmed the formation of the MIP on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the molar ratio of template to monomer
ce:section.ce:para: So many factors in MIP preparation steps are expected to influence the voltammetric response of the prepared electrode toward SDM molecules [39,40]. One of the most important parameters which affect the MIP electrode response to the analyte is the template/monomer ratio. Accordingly, the effect of the template/monomer ratio on the performance of the MIP was studied. The thickness of the deposit should be proportional to the concentration of the monomer in the polymeric matrix. The recognition sites' quantity and quality on the MIP surface are related to the monomer-template interactions in the pre-polymerization mixture [41].

To determine the effect of the template to monomer concentration on the response of MIP to SDM, a series of experiments were performed. Different SDM to aniline ratios, i.e., 1:1, 1:2, 2:3, 3:4, 1:3 and 1:4 were examined (Fig. S1(A)) in the pre-polymerization solution and subsequently, the MIP film was formed by CV between 0.0 and 1.4 V. The difference of the peak current (ΔI) of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−in the absence and in the presence of the SDM molecules was recorded. The experiments showed that ΔI was increased by increasing the template to monomer ratio up to 3:4 (Fig. S1(A)). So the optimal ratio was chosen as 3:4 for further optimization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the mass ratio of template molecule to ZIF-8
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of the template/ZIF-8 mass ratio was evaluated by changing this parameter from 1:0 to 1:3 in the MIP fabrication procedure. The results in Fig. S1(B) showed that by increasing the MOF, the response of the MIP electrode increased drastically up to the 1:0.5 mass ratio of SDM: ZIF-8. The increase of response might be due to the increasing mass transfer [24]. At higher amounts of the MOF, the response (ΔI) decreases revealing that the optimized amount of this parameter is 1:0.5. The decrease in the response might be because of the lack of template molecules to produce enough recognition sites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the number of electropolymerization cycles
ce:section.ce:para: The number of CV cycles used to form the polymer on the surface of the GQD/GCE and its effect on ΔI was investigated. For this purpose, the number of cycles was studied from two to twelve while other measurement conditions were kept constant (Fig. S1(C)). The plot of ΔI versus the number of CV cycles showed that ΔI increased up to eight cycles for DPV measurements. After that, by increasing the number of cycles, ΔI was decreased. The decrease of response by increasing the number of CV cycles over eight could be attributed to the reduction of MIP film conductivity. The consequent thickening of the polymer film causes the prevention of entering analyte molecules in the recognition cavities [11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of desorption time and rebinding time
ce:section.ce:para: To deplete the analyte/template from the recognition cavities, 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution was used and the electrode was immersed in it. The electrode response was increased drastically by increasing desorption time up to ten minutes (Fig. S1(D)). The behavior of the MIP film to remain unchanged for the longer periods of desorption could be the consequence of bare ready-to-bind recognition sites after ten minutes. So, the desorption time was set to be ten minutes.

The effect of rebinding time was studied by increasing the incubation time of the electrode in the SDM standard solution. The results of Fig. S1(E) indicated that the electrode response was increased up to 300 s and longer rebinding times showed no significant changes in ΔI. This behavior of the MIP film could be due to the occupation of binding sites with the SDM molecules. The rebinding time of 300 s was chosen as the optimized value of the parameter.

As the concentration of SDM molecules in [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− solution increases, we expected the DPV peak current to decrease. Fig. 3 (A) demonstrates the DPV curves of 0.0 to 275 nM SDM solutions that were recorded using the proposed method. The SDM quantitative analysis was performed using the calibration curves (Fig. 3(B) and (C)) by plotting ΔI versus concentration of the analyte. SDM showed a linear behavior from 0.01 to 1.0 nM by equation ΔI = 30.528CSDM + 26.365 with the squared correlation coefficient of 0.9926 and from 1.0 to 275 nM by equation ΔI = 0.3661CSDM + 62.468 with the squared correlation coefficient of 0.9903. Where ΔI and C are the difference of the peak current (µA) of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− in the absence and in the presence of the analyte and SDM concentration (nM), respectively. Limit of detection (LOD) was calculated based on three times of blank standard deviation divided to the calibration curve slope (3Sd/m) for six replicate of blank measurements and found to be 0.0063 nM.

The reproducibility of the modified sensor was assessed by measuring a 10 nM SDM solution using six electrodes which were made individually under the obtained optimal conditions and the corresponding ΔI as the response was recorded for six successive measurements (Fig. S2(A)). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was evaluated to be 2.43 % which is an acceptable value and shows the precision of the fabricated sensor.

To evaluate the effect of duration on the sensor performance, it was refrigerated (4 ˚C) and in a fixed interval (every-three days up to 30 days), the response of the electrode was recorded for a 10 nM SDM solution (Fig. S2(B)). The electrode response demonstrated good stability by an approximate reduction of 4.46 % for ΔI in a month.

The practicality of the fabricated sensor was studied by comparison of the proposed method with some other approaches that had been used to determine SDM. The method properties for this work and the alternatives are tabulated in Table S1. As could be seen, the proposed sensor demonstrates an acceptable performance and could be used to determine SDM as a reliable analytical tool.

The ability for selective determination of GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE toward SDM molecules was studied. For this purpose selectivity measurements were performed in a solution containing 50 nM SDM and separate solutions of 200 nM of interfering molecules i.e., sulfamethoxazole (SMX), chloramphenicol (CP), florfenicol (FOL), thiamphenicol (TAP), chlortetracycline (CTC) and streptomycin (STC). The ΔI for MIP and NIP was obtained in each mentioned solution and imprinting factor (IF = ΔIMIP/ΔINIP) was calculated. The results are shown in Fig. 4 (A). The studied potential interfering compounds consisted of molecules with similar structures to SDM and/or several hydrogen bonding groups which make them attractive to the recognition sites. The significantly greater IF value (13.87) for the SDM solution in comparison with the others shows the ability of the synthesized MIP to perform a selective determination of SDM. The response of the sensor for SDM was also evaluated in the presence of the mentioned potential interfering molecules (Fig. 4(B)). As could be seen, there is no significant change in the electrode response in the studied media.

To examine the usability of the proposed sensor, some real matrixes in which SDM might be present such as milk and river water were analyzed under the optimal conditions. The preparation of the samples is described in Section 2.6. After that four SDM concentrations (0.10, 1.0, 10.0 and 100 nM) were spiked to appropriate volumes of pretreated samples. Recovery of SDM was obtained by three measurements at each spiked concentration for milk and river water. The results are listed in Table 1 . As it could be seen there is a good accuracy and precision for the determination of SDM using GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE in real sample matrixes. Therefore, the proposed sensor could be used for the analysis of SDM in real samples such as dairy products and water.

In this work, GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE was successfully synthesized by electrochemical polymerization of aniline as the functional monomer in presence of SDM template. The GCE was modified by using GQD-ZIF-8 nanocomposite to improve electron transfer by increasing the active surface area of the electrode. The obtained recoveries in SDM measurements showed successful electrode application in real sample analysis i.e., milk and water. The greater IF value than the rest presented molecules exhibited that the GQD/ZIF-8/MIP/GCE could be used for selective determination of SDM without any serious interference of the studied media. The mentioned advantages along with the benefits arising from measurements by the modified electrochemical sensors make the proposed method potentially appropriate for analysis of other antibiotics in the environmental and foodstuff samples using various nanocomposite modifiers.

Behnaz Hatamluyi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Samira Sadeghzadeh: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zakiyeh Balouch Zehi: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Majid Rezayi: Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Seyedeh Belin Tavakoly Sany: Supervision, Project administration, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Deputy of Research, Mashhad University of Medical Sciences (Grant No. 971542), Mashhad, Iran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108266.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Furunculosis: results of Field Trials for Therapy with Ro5-0037, a Potentiated Sulfonamide
